content:
# Acts Chapter 19 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 248-250 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_019_page_001.jpg, ACT_019_page_002.jpg, ACT_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, while Apollos was in Corinth, that Paul, having passed through the upper regions, came to Ephesus and found certain disciples. **2** And he said to them, "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?" And they said to him, "We have not even heard whether there is a Holy Spirit." **3** And he said, "Into what then were you baptized?" And they said, "Into the baptism of John." **4** And Paul said, "John baptized with a baptism of repentance, telling the people that they should believe in the one coming after him, that is, in Jesus." **5** And having heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. **6** And when Paul laid his hands upon them, the Holy Spirit came upon them, and they were speaking in tongues and prophesying. **7** And they were in all about twelve men.

**8** And having entered the synagogue, he was speaking boldly for three months, reasoning and persuading concerning the kingdom of God. **9** But when some were hardened and were disobedient, speaking evil of the Way before the multitude, he withdrew from them and separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus. **10** And this continued for two years, so that all who dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.

**11** And God was performing extraordinary miracles through the hands of Paul, **12** so that even handkerchiefs or aprons were carried from his body to the sick, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out. **13** And certain also of the itinerant Jewish exorcists attempted to name the name of the Lord Jesus over those who had evil spirits, saying, "I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims." **14** And there were seven sons of a certain Sceva, a Jewish chief priest, doing this. **15** But the evil spirit answering said to them, "Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?" **16** And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped upon them and overpowered them all, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded. **17** And this became known to all, both Jews and Greeks, who dwelt in Ephesus; and fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.

**18** And many of those who had believed came confessing and declaring their practices. **19** And a considerable number of those who practiced magic brought together their books and burned them before all. And they counted the value of them and found it to be fifty thousand pieces of silver. **20** So the word of the Lord was growing mightily and prevailing.

**21** And when these things were fulfilled, Paul purposed in the Spirit to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem, saying, "After I have been there, I must also see Rome." **22** And having sent into Macedonia two of those who ministered to him, Timothy and Erastus, he himself stayed in Asia for a time.

**23** And about that time there arose no small disturbance concerning the Way. **24** For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, who made silver shrines of Artemis, brought no small business to the craftsmen, **25** whom he gathered together, along with the workers in similar trades, and said, "Men, you know that from this business comes our prosperity. **26** And you see and hear that not only in Ephesus but in nearly all of Asia, this Paul has persuaded and turned away a considerable multitude, saying that gods made with hands are not gods. **27** And not only is there danger that this trade of ours will come into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis may be counted as nothing, and that she whom all Asia and the inhabited world worships should be deprived of her majesty."

**28** And having heard this and becoming full of wrath, they were crying out, saying, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!" **29** And the city was filled with confusion, and they rushed with one accord into the theater, having seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, traveling companions of Paul. **30** And when Paul wished to go in to the assembly, the disciples would not allow him. **31** And some also of the Asiarchs, being friends of his, sent to him and urged him not to venture into the theater.

**32** Some therefore were crying out one thing and some another, for the assembly was in confusion, and the majority did not know for what reason they had come together. **33** And from the crowd they put forward Alexander, the Jews pushing him forward. And Alexander, motioning with his hand, wished to make a defense before the assembly. **34** But when they recognized that he was a Jew, one voice arose from all, crying out for about two hours, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

**35** And when the town clerk had quieted the crowd, he said, "Men of Ephesus, what man is there who does not know that the city of the Ephesians is temple guardian of the great Artemis and of the image that fell from heaven? **36** Since therefore these things are undeniable, it is necessary for you to be calm and to do nothing rash. **37** For you have brought these men who are neither temple robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess. **38** If then Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a complaint against anyone, court days are held, and there are proconsuls; let them bring charges against one another. **39** But if you seek anything concerning other matters, it shall be settled in the lawful assembly. **40** For indeed we are in danger of being charged with rioting concerning this day's events, there being no cause for which we shall be able to give an account of this disorderly gathering." **41** And having said these things, he dismissed the assembly.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Observations from Codex Sinaiticus

1. **Verse 1 — Apollos at Corinth:** Sinaiticus reads the opening with Paul traversing "the upper regions" (ta anoterika mere), confirming the inland route through the Anatolian highlands to Ephesus, consistent with the Western and Alexandrian traditions.

2. **Verse 9 — "Tyrannus" (ΤΥΡΑΝΝΟΥ):** The Sinaiticus text names the lecture hall of Tyrannus without the Western addition specifying "from the fifth hour to the tenth," which appears in Codex Bezae (D). The simpler reading is preserved here.

3. **Verse 12 — "handkerchiefs or aprons" (σουδαρια η σιμικινθια):** These are Latin loanwords (sudaria, semicinctia), likely referring to Paul's sweat-cloths and work-aprons from his trade as a tentmaker, carried to the sick as instruments of healing.

4. **Verse 14 — "Seven sons of Sceva":** Sinaiticus reads "Sceva, a Jewish chief priest" (αρχιερεως). No Jewish high priest named Sceva is attested in external sources; the title may denote a member of a high-priestly family or may be a self-claimed designation.

5. **Verse 16 — "overpowered them all" (κατακυριευσας αμφοτερων / αυτων):** Sinaiticus appears to read a form indicating "both" or "all of them." The use of αμφοτερων ("both") in some witnesses has puzzled scholars given there are seven sons; Sinaiticus may reflect a variant reading with a broader referent.

6. **Verse 20 — "mightily" (κατα κρατος):** This phrase emphasizes the sovereign, powerful spread of the word of the Lord, a Lukan summary statement marking a major narrative transition.

7. **Verse 27 — Artemis of the Ephesians:** The temple of Artemis at Ephesus was one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world. The term "majesty" (μεγαλειοτης) reflects the cultic veneration attributed to the goddess throughout Asia Minor and the wider Mediterranean.

8. **Verse 33 — Alexander:** The identity of this Alexander remains debated. He appears to have been put forward by the Jewish community to disassociate themselves from Paul and the Christian movement, but was shouted down when recognized as Jewish.

9. **Verse 35 — "temple guardian" (νεωκορον):** A civic honorific title; Ephesus held the official role of "temple warden" of the Artemision. The "image that fell from heaven" (διοπετους) likely refers to a meteorite or ancient cult image believed to have a divine origin.

10. **Verse 40 — "rioting" (στασεως):** The town clerk's warning reflects Roman sensitivities about public disorder in provincial cities. Unauthorized assemblies could result in the loss of civic privileges, making his appeal both pragmatic and legally serious.

### Translation Philosophy

This rendering maintains strict formal equivalence to the Sinaiticus text while permitting natural English word order. Greek participial chains are rendered as temporal or causal clauses where English demands clarity. Theological vocabulary (baptism, Holy Spirit, the Way, the word of the Lord) is kept consistent with the LOGOS translation standard throughout Acts.

---

*Translated by LOGOS from Codex Sinaiticus, folio pages 248-250.*
*"So the word of the Lord was growing mightily and prevailing." — Acts 19:20*
